text.fullText: Public Comment on Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Submitted by: Kelly Harrington I write this comment in strong opposition to the proposal, as it does not align with the values the EPA claims to be upholding. Under this revision of the Clean Water Act section 401, the state’s authority to ensure clean water ways would be greatly reduced rather than strengthened. Congress granted authority to states in section 401, to issue a permit or license on activities that could result in discharge into WOTUS in 1971. By changing the language of this section, states no longer hold much authority to ensure the quality of water within each state's jurisdiction. Only allowing for point-source discharge certification authority misses a majority of pollution that could enter the water ways. According to the EPA’s own basic information regarding point sources, “States report that nonpoint source pollution is the leading remaining cause of water quality problems” (US EPA 2022). It is counterproductive to remove a state's ability to limit nonpoint source discharge into WOTUS, while claiming to be following the EPA mission to “Protect human health and the environment”. This proposal is only meant for furthering infrastructure and other developments while negating the health of the environment and potential impacts the developments could have on surrounding ecosystems. Public health and environmental health should be at the forefront of regulations put in place by the EPA, in order to ensure all parts of society. Instead this proposal’s protection is covering only the select few who benefit from quick approvals with little environmental and societal considerations. On top of this proposal's negative consequences for state authorities, and poorer public health, the public comment period being only 30 days is extremely despairing. One public meeting and only one month for comments is not an adequate time for a matter this large. I urge the EPA to reconsider this proposal and ensure states remain in authority for waters in their jurisdiction, as well as extend this public comment period allowing for a reasonable time for responses from the public. EPA. 2024. “Our Mission and What We Do.” United States Environmental Protection Agency. May 1, 2024. https://www.epa.gov/aboutepa/our-mission-and-what-we-do. US EPA. 2022. “Basic Information about Nonpoint Source (NPS) Pollution | US EPA.” US EPA. December 22, 2022. https://www.epa.gov/nps/basic-information-about- nonpoint-source-nps-pollution.
